summary: Gruber speculates extensively about Apple's rumored tablet device, arguing that the key question is why anyone who already owns an iPhone and a MacBook would want one. He dismisses the notion that it's merely a big iPod Touch, an e-reader, or a couch-surfing device, instead arguing Apple is 'swinging big' to redefine personal computing. He predicts the tablet will run a new OS derived from the same core as iPhone OS and Mac OS X but with a bespoke UI designed for its form factor. Gruber concludes the tablet will eventually be bought instead of a MacBook, serving as Apple's reconception of portable general-purpose computing — doing less than a Mac but doing those things insanely well.
key_points: The central question for the tablet is justifying its existence between iPhone and MacBook — Apple won't ship it without a compelling answer
The tablet won't simply be a scaled-up iPhone OS; Apple will design a new interaction model because a 7-inch+ display can't be used one-handed like an iPhone
Apple is positioning the tablet as a general-purpose computing device, not a dedicated e-reader or media player — it's a matter of 'and' not 'or'
Long-term, the tablet will be to the MacBook what the Macintosh was to the Apple II — a fundamental reconception of personal computing
Apple's secrecy around the project is complete, rivaling the pre-announcement iPhone era, with even publishing industry partners shown no hardware or software
money_quotes: If you already have an iPhone and a MacBook; why would you want this?
I say they're swinging big — redefining the experience of personal computing.
It will offer a fraction of the functionality of a MacBook — but that fraction will be way more fun.

The same myopic feature-checklist-obsessed critics who dismissed the iPhone will focus on all that The Tablet doesn't do and declare that this time, Apple really has fucked up but good. The rest of us will get in line to buy one.
If you think Apple is making a dedicated device for reading e-books and articles, you're thinking too small.
It will be to the MacBook what the Macintosh was to the Apple II.
Apple is not in the business of making monolithic OSes that they cram down your throat on as many widely-varying devices as possible.
If you're thinking The Tablet is just a big iPhone, or just Apple's take on the e-reader, or just a media player, or just anything, I say you're thinking too small.
key_insight: The tablet's success depends not on being a bigger iPhone or a smaller Mac, but on Apple reconceiving what personal portable computing means — doing fewer things but doing them so well that it replaces the laptop for most people's daily use.
